frozen_components: base instruction-tuned LLM parameters (except embeddings for abstract tokens)
architecture.summary: Abstract Chain-of-Thought augments decoder-only LLMs with a reserved vocabulary of abstract tokens that function as discrete latent reasoning traces. During inference, the model generates a bounded sequence of abstract tokens (instead of natural language CoT) before producing a response. Information flows: prompt → abstract token sequence (constrained decoding) → response. The abstract tokens are trained via a two-stage pipeline: (1) policy iteration warm-up alternating between bottlenecked SFT (where response attends only to prompt and abstract tokens, not verbal CoT) and self-distillation without verbal guidance, and (2) warm-started RL (GRPO with constrained decoding) to refine abstract sequence generation.
trained_components: abstract token embeddings (randomly initialized, learned during warm-up and RL)
abstract sequence generation policy (constrained to reserved vocabulary)
response generation conditioned on abstract tokens

biblio.title: Thinking Without Words: Efficient Latent Reasoning with Abstract Chain-of-Thought

claims.statement: Abstract-CoT achieves up to 11.6× fewer reasoning tokens while demonstrating comparable performance across mathematical reasoning, instruction-following, and multi-hop reasoning
On MATH-500 with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 90.8% accuracy with 144 tokens, compared to SFT + RL baseline of 92.6% accuracy with 1671 tokens
On AlpacaEval with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 60.8 win-rate with 225 tokens, outperforming SFT + RL baseline of 58.4 win-rate with 496 tokens
On HotpotQA with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 58.8 F1 with 171 tokens, compared to SFT + RL baseline of 58.1 F1 with 735 tokens

baseline.name: verbal CoT (SFT + RL) for each model

claims.statement: Abstract-CoT generalizes across model families: on MATH-500, Qwen3-4B achieves 89.8% accuracy with 141 tokens and Granite 4.0 Micro (3B) achieves 74.4% accuracy with 153 tokens, both substantially reducing tokens compared to their verbal CoT baselines

An emergent power law distribution over the abstract vocabulary is observed during RL training, evolving across the training phases, with a single dominant token leveraged across diverse settings and token usage converging to a Zipf-like distribution
Performing cold-start RL without warm-up is insufficient and often underperforms the base instruction-tuned model; warm-up alone lags behind SFT with verbal CoT on most benchmarks

Permutation ablation shows that abstract sequences exhibit learned compositional behavior: permuting abstract tokens at the token level reduces performance, with smaller performance drops than for verbal CoT permutation, indicating discrete ordering structure in the abstract reasoning language
